questions.question: The Brown & Stanley Safety Planning model includes six steps. Which of the following is the correct sequence?

options.text: Professional resources, warning signs, safe people, coping strategies, distractions, environment
Warning signs, internal coping strategies, social contacts for distraction, people to ask for help, professionals and crisis resources, making your environment safer
Medication plan, therapist contact, warning signs, coping strategies, safe places, crisis numbers
Warning signs, avoidance strategies, safe places, medications, professional resources, environment

questions.explanation: The six-step safety plan follows this sequence: (1) Warning signs, (2) Internal coping strategies you can do alone, (3) Social contacts for distraction, (4) People you can ask for direct help, (5) Professionals and crisis resources (988, Crisis Text Line, therapist), and (6) Making your environment safer.

questions.question: Why does the lesson recommend creating a safety plan when you're relatively stable rather than during a crisis?

options.text: Because safety plans are only effective if written on paper, not on a phone
Because during a crisis your prefrontal cortex may not be fully online, making it hard to think clearly enough to figure out what to do
Because therapists require a pre-written safety plan before beginning treatment
Because crisis situations only last a few seconds and there isn't time to write

questions.explanation: During a crisis, you may not be able to think clearly enough to figure out what to do — your prefrontal cortex (thinking brain) may be offline while your amygdala (alarm system) is activated. Having a written plan removes that barrier and gives you concrete steps to follow when clear thinking is hardest.

questions.question: According to the lesson, what does building a support network look like when trust feels difficult?

options.text: Force yourself to share your full story with multiple people immediately
Avoid all social connection until you feel completely healed
Start small with low-risk connections, be selective about what you share, and test trust gradually
Only rely on professional therapists — personal relationships are too risky

questions.explanation: The lesson advises starting small (one person is enough), using low-risk connections first (online support groups, helplines), being selective about who knows your story, testing trust gradually (share a little and see how the person responds), and allowing imperfection. Building support is a gradual process, not an all-or-nothing effort.

questions.question: What is post-traumatic growth, and how should it be understood?

options.text: It is the expected and required outcome of trauma recovery that all survivors should aim for
It means being grateful for the trauma because it made you stronger
It is positive psychological changes that some people experience from the struggle with trauma — possible but not required, and not about being grateful for the trauma
It is a medical diagnosis that indicates PTSD has been fully cured

questions.explanation: Post-traumatic growth refers to positive psychological changes that can emerge from the struggle with a highly challenging life event. The lesson emphasizes that it is NOT required, NOT the goal of recovery, and NOT about being 'grateful' for trauma. Some people find meaning; others do not. Both paths are completely valid.

questions.question: The lesson describes different support roles people can fill. Which role provides companionship and helps you 'get out of your head'?

options.text: Listener
Professional
Practical helper
Distracter

questions.explanation: The lesson identifies five support roles: Listener (hears you without trying to fix things), Distracter (helps you get out of your head — a friend who makes you laugh), Practical helper (assists with tasks when you're overwhelmed), Understanding person (knows about your trauma and 'gets it'), and Professional (provides clinical support). You don't need one person to fill all roles.

title: Building Safety and Support Systems - Quiz
